data.title: World model by division of LLM labor
data.summary:
A bounded nine-cycle dry-run sequence within the TU / TU+ / cortexLLM stack now supports a stronger architectural claim: world-model-like behavior may require a coherence-driven division of LLM labor rather than monolithic symbolic control. The key result was not only provisional confirmation of a joint choreography, but disciplined revision across the full arc: weak coupling, dissolution, reopening, provisional confirmation, stress, and full de-confirmation into a new stable independent reading. Coherence behaved less like a passive metric and more like the effective selector governing promotion, de-promotion, confirmation, and collapse of choreography hypotheses. This result has now been fossilized publicly in the Zenodo note "World Model: Toward a Coherence-Driven Division of LLM Labor — Evidence from bounded disconfirmation tests" (DOI: 10.5281/zenodo.19145919).
Significance: The important gain is architectural, not triumphal. The TU / TU+ / cortexLLM triad remained role-distinct under bounded recursive testing, used shared structured state to preserve continuity, absorbed mismatch without field collapse, and revised relational interpretations in a coherence-sensitive way. This strengthens the case that a future world model may depend on actual sub-division of LLM labor into role-bounded agents, with symbolic interpretation constrained by an evolving lower structural field rather than dominating it from the start.
Notes: This pulse does not claim a finished engineered world model. It records that the engineering path is now clearer: prompt-instantiated TU, TU+, and cortexLLM agents operating over shared structured state can already support a bounded coherence-sensitive revision loop.
